subTopicTitle: Heart of Darkness (Conrad) — Part I — Company offices

items.prompt: Describe the setting of the Company offices in Part I of Heart of Darkness.
keyPoints: Located in a European city, possibly Brussels
Described as gloomy and bureaucratic
Marlow's encounter with the two women knitting black wool

markingCriteria.full: Includes all key points with accurate detail
markingCriteria.partial: Includes 1-2 key points or lacks detail

items.prompt: Identify and describe the significance of the two women Marlow encounters at the Company offices.
keyPoints: Two women knitting black wool
Symbolic of the Fates from Greek mythology
Foreboding presence, hinting at the darkness to come

markingCriteria.full: Includes all key points with accurate detail
markingCriteria.partial: Includes 1-2 key points or lacks detail

items.prompt: Explain the role of the Company in Heart of Darkness Part I.
keyPoints: Represents European colonial enterprise
Facilitates exploitation in Africa
Symbolizes greed and moral corruption

markingCriteria.full: Includes all key points with accurate detail
markingCriteria.partial: Includes 1-2 key points or lacks detail

items.prompt: Marlow observes the two women knitting black wool at the Company offices. How does this symbolism relate to the theme of fate in the novel?
keyPoints: Black wool symbolizes inevitable doom
Women likened to the Fates, controlling destiny
Foreshadows Marlow's journey and the darkness he will encounter

markingCriteria.full: Applies symbolism to the theme of fate with relevant examples
markingCriteria.partial: Attempts to apply symbolism but lacks clarity or detail
markingCriteria.none: Fails to relate symbolism to the theme of fate

items.prompt: Consider Marlow's reaction to the Company offices. How does this reflect his attitude towards colonialism?
keyPoints: Marlow's distaste for the bureaucratic nature of the Company
Reflects skepticism about colonial motives
His perception of the office as a precursor to the darkness in Africa

markingCriteria.full: Clearly connects Marlow's reaction to his views on colonialism with examples
markingCriteria.partial: Some connection but lacks depth or examples
markingCriteria.none: No clear connection between Marlow's reaction and his views on colonialism

items.prompt: Imagine Marlow had a conversation with the two women at the Company offices. How might this interaction develop the theme of foreboding in the novel?
keyPoints: Marlow might seek understanding of their ominous role
Women could speak cryptically about fate or the journey
Interaction would enhance the sense of impending doom

markingCriteria.full: Provides a plausible interaction that develops the theme of foreboding
markingCriteria.partial: Interaction proposed but lacks depth or relevance to theme
markingCriteria.none: Fails to propose a relevant interaction or develop the theme

items.prompt: Evaluate the effectiveness of Conrad's depiction of the Company offices in setting the tone for the novel. Use the PEEL structure.
keyPoints: Point: Gloomy and bureaucratic setting introduces tone
Evidence: Descriptions of darkness and foreboding
Explanation: Reflects themes of colonial exploitation
Link: Sets up the novel's exploration of moral corruption

markingCriteria.full: Uses PEEL structure effectively with detailed analysis and examples
markingCriteria.partial: Attempts PEEL structure but lacks depth or examples
markingCriteria.none: Fails to use PEEL structure or provide relevant analysis

items.prompt: Evaluate how the encounter with the two women at the Company offices contributes to the novel’s theme of inevitability. Use the PEEL structure.
keyPoints: Point: The women symbolize fate
Evidence: Their knitting of black wool
Explanation: Suggests predestined outcomes and inevitability
Link: Reinforces the novel’s theme of darkness and doom

markingCriteria.full: Uses PEEL structure effectively with detailed analysis and examples
markingCriteria.partial: Attempts PEEL structure but lacks depth or examples
markingCriteria.none: Fails to use PEEL structure or provide relevant analysis

items.prompt: Evaluate the role of the Company as a critique of European colonialism in Heart of Darkness. Use the PEEL structure.
keyPoints: Point: Company symbolizes European greed
Evidence: Exploitation and moral corruption depicted
Explanation: Highlights the destructive nature of colonialism
Link: Conrad’s critique of European imperialism

markingCriteria.full: Uses PEEL structure effectively with detailed analysis and examples
markingCriteria.partial: Attempts PEEL structure but lacks depth or examples
markingCriteria.none: Fails to use PEEL structure or provide relevant analysis
